24|CMS|The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is the federal agency responsible for administering and overseeing the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit program. As the primary regulatory body for Medicare Part D, CMS manages the entire lifecycle of Medicare Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs), including contract management, bid review, and the approval of plan bids and service areas. CMS oversees the contracting process with PDP Sponsors, ensuring that plans meet federal requirements before entering or renewing contracts. The agency is also responsible for approving formulary licensing, setting enrollment rules, and establishing reimbursement and reporting standards for PDPs.

CMS plays a critical role in ensuring compliance with federal regulations under 42 CFR Part 423, enforcing contractual obligations, and monitoring plan performance through complaint tracking and regulatory actions. The agency conducts regular audits and reviews to ensure that PDPs adhere to quality, financial, and operational standards. Additionally, CMS is responsible for the attestation of benefit plans and the renewal of contracts, ensuring that plans continue to meet federal benchmarks and serve beneficiaries effectively.

In its oversight capacity, CMS releases public data related to Part D payments, medical loss ratios, and plan performance, promoting transparency and accountability. The agency also manages contract terminations and approvals for notices, ensuring that changes to plan offerings or sponsorships are properly vetted and compliant with federal law. Through its comprehensive regulatory framework, CMS ensures that the Medicare Part D program remains accessible, affordable, and aligned with federal policy objectives. Overall, CMS serves as the central authority for the administration, regulation, and continuous improvement of the Medicare Part D program, safeguarding the interests of beneficiaries and maintaining the integrity of the prescription drug benefit system.|46
17|ADMINISTRATOR OF CMS|The official within the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services responsible for making determinations regarding the eligibility of entities to operate Medicare Prescription Drug Plans. This individual holds authority under 42 CFR § 423.503 to approve or deny PDP Sponsor status.|1
25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157
6|MEDICARE PRESCRIPTION DRUG BENEFIT MANUAL|The Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit Manual is a comprehensive guidance document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that provides detailed instructions on the administration and implementation of the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit. This manual serves as a critical resource for stakeholders involved in the Medicare Part D program, including plan sponsors, pharmacies, pharmacies, billing entities, and other administrative partners. It outlines the operational framework necessary for the effective and compliant delivery of prescription drug coverage to Medicare beneficiaries.

The manual covers a wide range of topics essential to the functioning of the Part D program, including plan operations, claims processing, formulary management, drug pricing, beneficiary eligibility, and compliance standards. It provides detailed guidance on how plans must structure their benefits, manage drug tiers and cost-sharing, and ensure timely and accurate claims adjudication. Additionally, it establishes requirements for plan sponsors to maintain data integrity, ensure beneficiary access to covered drugs, and adhere to federal regulations and CMS policies.

The document also addresses key compliance and oversight mechanisms, including reporting obligations, audit procedures, and enforcement protocols. It ensures that all entities participating in the Medicare Part D program operate in alignment with federal law and CMS directives, promoting consistency, transparency, and accountability across the system. By standardizing procedures and expectations, the Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit Manual helps maintain the integrity of the program and supports the delivery of safe, affordable, and accessible prescription medications to millions of Medicare beneficiaries.

Overall, the manual is an authoritative reference that supports the seamless administration of the Medicare Part D program, ensuring that all aspects—from plan design to claims processing—are conducted in a uniform, compliant, and beneficiary-focused manner. Its role in guiding plan operations and compliance underscores its importance as a foundational document in the U.S. Medicare system.|2
60|SUMMARY RECONCILED PART D PAYMENT DATA|A public dataset released by CMS after reconciliation of Part D payments, as mandated by 42 CFR § 423.505(o)(1), used to ensure transparency in fund distribution.|1
7|MEDICARE COMMUNICATIONS AND MARKETING GUIDELINES|The Medicare Communications and Marketing Guidelines are federal regulations issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that govern how Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors may communicate with and market their plans to beneficiaries. These guidelines are designed to ensure transparency, accuracy, and compliance with established consumer protection standards. By setting clear parameters for marketing activities, the guidelines aim to protect beneficiaries from misleading or deceptive information, enabling them to make informed decisions about their prescription drug coverage. The rules apply specifically to PDP Sponsors, who are responsible for adhering to these standards when developing and disseminating marketing materials, conducting outreach, and engaging with Medicare beneficiaries through various channels, including print, digital, and in-person communications. The guidelines emphasize truthful, balanced, and easily understandable information, requiring that all plan benefits, costs, formulary details, and coverage limitations be presented clearly and without exaggeration. Additionally, the regulations prohibit the use of tactics that could mislead or manipulate beneficiaries, such as omitting key information, using confusing terminology, or making unsubstantiated claims. CMS enforces these guidelines to maintain the integrity of the Medicare Part D program and to promote equitable access to accurate health information. Compliance with the Medicare Communications and Marketing Guidelines is mandatory for all PDP Sponsors, and failure to adhere may result in penalties or corrective actions. Overall, the guidelines serve as a critical framework for ensuring that marketing efforts are ethical, compliant, and focused on the best interests of Medicare beneficiaries.|2

11|CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES|The CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES are official documents issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to assist plan sponsors, beneficiaries, and other stakeholders in understanding and complying with the requirements of the Medicare Part D program. These guides serve as essential resources for PDP (Prescription Drug Plan) Sponsors and other participants, offering detailed explanations of operational procedures, policy interpretations, and regulatory expectations. Designed to promote consistency and clarity, the CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES provide authoritative guidance on program administration, including enrollment processes, formulary management, drug pricing, coverage determinations, and appeals procedures. They also address key compliance issues, helping sponsors navigate complex regulatory frameworks and ensure adherence to federal standards. By clarifying CMS policies and procedures, these documents support accurate implementation of Medicare Part D benefits, reduce administrative errors, and enhance the overall effectiveness of the prescription drug program. The guides are regularly updated to reflect changes in legislation, regulations, and program operations, ensuring that stakeholders have access to current and reliable information. As official CMS publications, the CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES are recognized as authoritative sources for interpreting Medicare Part D rules and are widely used by plan sponsors, pharmacy networks, healthcare providers, and beneficiaries alike. Their comprehensive nature makes them indispensable tools for maintaining program integrity, improving beneficiary access to medications, and supporting the smooth functioning of the Medicare Part D framework.|2

0|CENTERS FOR MEDICARE & MEDICAID SERVICES|The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is a U.S. federal agency responsible for administering Medicare, Medicaid, and the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP). As a key component of the Department of Health and Human Services, CMS oversees the implementation and regulation of these critical health programs, ensuring they meet federal standards and serve millions of Americans. PDP Sponsor agrees to provide notice based on best knowledge, information, and belief to CMS of any integrity items related to payments from governmental entities, both Federal and State, for healthcare or prescription drug services that would have been reported as part of Table A. of the Business Integrity section of the PDP application. These items include any investigations, legal actions or matters subject to arbitration brought involving the PDP Sponsor (or the PDP Sponsor's firm if applicable) and its subcontractors (excluding contracted network providers), including any key management or executive staff, or any major shareholders (5% or more), by a government agency (State or Federal) on matters relating to payments from governmental entities, both Federal and State, for healthcare and/or prescription drug services. In providing the notice, the PDP Sponsor shall keep the Government informed of when the integrity item is initiated and when it is closed. Notice should be provided of the details concerning any resolution and monetary payments as well as any settlement agreements or corporate integrity agreements.
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to provide notice based on best knowledge, information, and belief to CMS in the event PDP Sponsor or any of its subcontractors is criminally convicted or has a civil judgment entered against it for fraudulent activities or is sanctioned under any Federal program involving the provision of health care or prescription drug services.

## L. LOW-INCOME SUBSIDY

PDP Sponsor agrees that it will participate in the administration of subsidies for low-income subsidy eligible individuals according to Subpart P of 42 CFR Part 423.

## M. COMMUNICATION WITH CMS

PDP Sponsor agrees that it shall maintain the capacity to communicate with CMS electronically in accordance with CMS requirements.

## N. BENEFICIARY FINANCIAL PROTECTIONS

PDP Sponsor agrees to afford its enrollees protection from liability for payment of fees that are the obligation of PDP Sponsor in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(g).

## O. RELATIONSHIP WITH FIRST TIER, DOWNSTREAM, AND RELATED ENTITIES

1. PDP Sponsor agrees that it maintains ultimate responsibility for adhering to and otherwise fully complying with all terms and conditions of this contract with CMS. [42 CFR § 423.505(i)]

results.question: What is the claim submission window for a pharmacy located in a long-term care facility?
results.expected_answer: Not less than 30 days but not more than 90 days
results.evidence: pharmacy located in, or having a contract with, a long-term care facility will have not less than 30 days (but not more than 90 days) to submit claims
results.llm_answer: The claim submission window for a pharmacy located in a long-term care facility is up to 90 days.

8|FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS|The "FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS," a final guidance document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) in 2024, provides comprehensive updates and requirements for the redesign of the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit program for Contract Year (CY) 2025. This document serves as a critical roadmap for Part D plan sponsors, detailing structural and operational changes aimed at improving the program’s effectiveness, sustainability, and beneficiary outcomes. Key components of the redesign include significant updates to benefit structures, such as enhanced coverage for high-cost drugs, expanded access to preferred formulary tiers, and modifications to cost-sharing requirements to reduce out-of-pocket expenses for beneficiaries. These changes are designed to increase affordability and transparency in drug coverage while aligning with broader CMS goals for value-based care.

In addition to benefit structure reforms, the guidance outlines revised risk adjustment methodologies intended to more accurately reflect the health status and cost profiles of enrolled beneficiaries. These updated risk adjustment models aim to ensure fairer payment distributions among plan sponsors and incentivize the enrollment of higher-risk individuals. The document also introduces new and refined plan performance metrics, which will be used to assess and rate Part D plans based on quality, access, and financial stewardship. These metrics are expected to drive continuous improvement in plan performance and accountability.

The final instructions also clarify sponsor responsibilities, emphasizing enhanced data reporting, compliance with new enrollment and disenrollment protocols, and strengthened oversight of pharmacy networks and formulary management. CMS underscores the importance of plan sponsors’ proactive engagement in implementing these changes, including timely updates to plan materials, beneficiary communications, and internal systems to ensure seamless transition into CY 2025.

Overall, the "FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS" represents a pivotal step in modernizing the Medicare Part D program, balancing beneficiary access and affordability with financial sustainability and quality improvement. By aligning benefit design, risk adjustment, performance measurement, and sponsor obligations, CMS aims to create a more equitable, efficient, and responsive prescription drug benefit system for millions of Medicare beneficiaries. The guidance reflects a coordinated effort to address longstanding challenges in the Part D program while preparing for future healthcare delivery models.|2

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

129|INTEGRITY ITEMS|INTEGRITY ITEMS refer to specific instances or findings that involve investigations, legal actions, or arbitration proceedings related to the Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor, its key personnel, subcontractors, or major shareholders. These matters are specifically tied to government payments for healthcare or prescription drug services and may indicate potential misconduct or non-compliance. Such integrity issues are of significant regulatory concern, particularly when they involve allegations of fraud, waste, or abuse in the context of federal healthcare programs. PDP Sponsors are required to disclose any reports or findings related to these integrity matters to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of their compliance obligations. This disclosure requirement ensures transparency and accountability in the administration of government-funded healthcare programs. The scope of INTEGRITY ITEMS encompasses not only direct actions against the PDP Sponsor but also any associated parties—such as subcontractors or major shareholders—whose actions may impact the integrity of the healthcare payment system. The obligation to report these items underscores the importance of maintaining ethical standards and operational compliance within the Medicare Part D program. By requiring timely and accurate disclosure, CMS aims to safeguard public funds, ensure fair distribution of benefits, and uphold the trustworthiness of the healthcare delivery system. Therefore, INTEGRITY ITEMS serve as a critical component of regulatory oversight, enabling CMS to monitor risks, take corrective actions when necessary, and enforce compliance across the PDP ecosystem.|1

122|GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES|GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES refer to federal and state-level agencies that play a critical role in the oversight and administration of healthcare and prescription drug programs in the United States. These entities are responsible for providing financial support through payments for healthcare services and prescription drug coverage, particularly within the context of Medicare Part D, where they work in coordination with Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors. As key stakeholders in the healthcare ecosystem, GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES are tasked with ensuring that public funds are used appropriately and in compliance with established regulations. They require PDP Sponsors to submit regular compliance reports, enabling transparency and accountability in the delivery of benefits to beneficiaries. In addition to funding, these entities possess regulatory authority and may initiate investigations or pursue legal actions against PDP Sponsors or their affiliated organizations if non-compliance, fraud, or improper practices are identified. This oversight function is essential to maintaining the integrity of federal and state healthcare programs. The scope of their authority spans both federal agencies—such as the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS)—and state-level departments responsible for administering Medicaid and state-specific health programs. Their dual role as funders and enforcers underscores their central position in shaping the operational and ethical standards of PDP Sponsors. By mandating compliance reporting and enforcing regulatory standards, GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES help safeguard public resources and ensure equitable access to healthcare and prescription medications. Their ability to intervene through investigations or legal proceedings reinforces the importance of adherence to federal and state laws, particularly in areas involving billing, data reporting, and beneficiary protections. Overall, GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES serve as vital regulators, financiers, and monitors within the healthcare system, ensuring that PDP Sponsors fulfill their obligations to beneficiaries while operating within a framework of accountability and transparency.|1

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

24|CMS|The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is the federal agency responsible for administering and overseeing the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit program. As the primary regulatory body for Medicare Part D, CMS manages the entire lifecycle of Medicare Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs), including contract management, bid review, and the approval of plan bids and service areas. CMS oversees the contracting process with PDP Sponsors, ensuring that plans meet federal requirements before entering or renewing contracts. The agency is also responsible for approving formulary licensing, setting enrollment rules, and establishing reimbursement and reporting standards for PDPs.

CMS plays a critical role in ensuring compliance with federal regulations under 42 CFR Part 423, enforcing contractual obligations, and monitoring plan performance through complaint tracking and regulatory actions. The agency conducts regular audits and reviews to ensure that PDPs adhere to quality, financial, and operational standards. Additionally, CMS is responsible for the attestation of benefit plans and the renewal of contracts, ensuring that plans continue to meet federal benchmarks and serve beneficiaries effectively.

In its oversight capacity, CMS releases public data related to Part D payments, medical loss ratios, and plan performance, promoting transparency and accountability. The agency also manages contract terminations and approvals for notices, ensuring that changes to plan offerings or sponsorships are properly vetted and compliant with federal law. Through its comprehensive regulatory framework, CMS ensures that the Medicare Part D program remains accessible, affordable, and aligned with federal policy objectives. id|text
3| CFR Part 423 Subpart V.

## D. QUALITY ASSURANCE/UTILIZATION MANAGEMENT

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to operate quality assurance, drug utilization management, drug management, and medication therapy management programs, and to support electronic prescribing, in accordance with Subpart D of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to address and resolve complaints received by CMS against the PDP Sponsor through the CMS complaint tracking system as required in 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(22).
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain a Part D summary plan rating score of at least 3 stars as required by 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(26).
4. PDP Sponsor agrees to pass an essential operations test prior to the start of the benefit year. This provision only applies to new sponsors that have not previously entered into a Part D contract with CMS and neither it, nor another subsidiary of the applicant's parent organization, is offering Part D benefits during the current year. [42 CFR § 423.505(b)(27)]

## E. APPEALS AND GRIEVANCES

PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all requirements in Subpart M of 42 CFR Part 423 governing coverage determinations, grievances and appeals, and formulary exceptions, and the applicable provisions of Subpart U.

## F. PAYMENT TO PDP SPONSOR

1. PDP Sponsor and CMS agree that payment under this contract is governed by the rules in Subpart G of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all applicable Federal laws and regulations, guidance, and authorities pertaining to claims and debt collections. In the event that the Government determines that PDP Sponsor has been overpaid, PDP Sponsor agrees to return those overpaid monies back to the Government.

## G. BID SUBMISSION AND REVIEW

If PDP Sponsor intends to participate in the Part D program for the next program year, PDP Sponsor agrees to submit the next year's bid, including all required information on premiums, benefits, and cost-sharing, by the applicable due date, as provided in Subpart F of 42 CFR Part 423 so that CMS and the PDP Sponsor may conduct negotiations regarding the terms and conditions of the proposed bid and benefit plan renewal.

## H. STATE LAW AND LICENSURE REQUIREMENTS

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with State law to the extent that it is not preempted by Federal law as described in Subpart I of 42 CFR Part 423.

8|FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS|The "FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS," a final guidance document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) in 2024, provides comprehensive updates and requirements for the redesign of the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit program for Contract Year (CY) 2025. This document serves as a critical roadmap for Part D plan sponsors, detailing structural and operational changes aimed at improving the program’s effectiveness, sustainability, and beneficiary outcomes. Key components of the redesign include significant updates to benefit structures, such as enhanced coverage for high-cost drugs, expanded access to preferred formulary tiers, and modifications to cost-sharing requirements to reduce out-of-pocket expenses for beneficiaries. These changes are designed to increase affordability and transparency in drug coverage while aligning with broader CMS goals for value-based care.

In addition to benefit structure reforms, the guidance outlines revised risk adjustment methodologies intended to more accurately reflect the health status and cost profiles of enrolled beneficiaries. These updated risk adjustment models aim to ensure fairer payment distributions among plan sponsors and incentivize the enrollment of higher-risk individuals. The document also introduces new and refined plan performance metrics, which will be used to assess and rate Part D plans based on quality, access, and financial stewardship. These metrics are expected to drive continuous improvement in plan performance and accountability.

The final instructions also clarify sponsor responsibilities, emphasizing enhanced data reporting, compliance with new enrollment and disenrollment protocols, and strengthened oversight of pharmacy networks and formulary management. CMS underscores the importance of plan sponsors’ proactive engagement in implementing these changes, including timely updates to plan materials, beneficiary communications, and internal systems to ensure seamless transition into CY 2025.

Overall, the "FINAL CY 2025 PART D REDESIGN PROGRAM INSTRUCTIONS" represents a pivotal step in modernizing the Medicare Part D program, balancing beneficiary access and affordability with financial sustainability and quality improvement. By aligning benefit design, risk adjustment, performance measurement, and sponsor obligations, CMS aims to create a more equitable, efficient, and responsive prescription drug benefit system for millions of Medicare beneficiaries. The guidance reflects a coordinated effort to address longstanding challenges in the Part D program while preparing for future healthcare delivery models.|2

6|MEDICARE PRESCRIPTION DRUG BENEFIT MANUAL|The Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit Manual is a comprehensive guidance document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that provides detailed instructions on the administration and implementation of the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit. This manual serves as a critical resource for stakeholders involved in the Medicare Part D program, including plan sponsors, pharmacies, pharmacies, billing entities, and other administrative partners. By standardizing procedures and expectations, the Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit Manual helps maintain the integrity of the program and supports the delivery of safe, affordable, and accessible prescription medications to millions of Medicare beneficiaries.

Overall, the manual is an authoritative reference that supports the seamless administration of the Medicare Part D program, ensuring that all aspects—from plan design to claims processing—are conducted in a uniform, compliant, and beneficiary-focused manner. Its role in guiding plan operations and compliance underscores its importance as a foundational document in the U.S. Medicare system.|2

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

This provision outlines the conditions under which a PDP Sponsor may elect not to renew its contract at the end of the contract term. Specifically, the regulation permits a PDP Sponsor to decline renewal, provided it complies with strict notification requirements. These requirements mandate that the sponsor must notify both the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) and Medicare enrollees of its intent not to renew the contract. The regulation specifies precise timing for such notifications, ensuring that enrollees are informed well in advance of the contract’s expiration to allow for adequate time to make alternative coverage decisions. Additionally, the content of the notice must meet defined standards, including clear communication of the non-renewal decision, the effective date of termination, and information about available enrollment options during the subsequent Medicare Open Enrollment Period. The purpose of these requirements is to protect the continuity of care for Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage. By enforcing timely and transparent communication, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1) ensures that both CMS and enrollees are adequately informed, enabling a smooth transition for affected individuals. The regulation thus balances the operational flexibility of PDP Sponsors with the critical need to safeguard beneficiary access to essential medications. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by detailing both the right to non-renewal and the mandatory procedural safeguards that must be followed. Together, these elements form a coherent framework that supports regulatory compliance, beneficiary protection, and the stability of the Medicare Part D program.|1
183|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the process by which a Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor must notify the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) of its intent not to renew a Medicare Part D contract. This regulation establishes a mandatory notification requirement, ensuring transparency and timely communication between PDP Sponsors and CMS regarding contract non-renewal. The rule specifies that a PDP Sponsor must submit formal notice to CMS by the first Monday of June each year, which serves as the deadline for communicating its decision not to renew the contract for the upcoming plan year. This timing allows CMS sufficient lead time to manage the transition process, ensure continuity of coverage for beneficiaries, and facilitate the necessary administrative and operational adjustments. The notice must include all required content as specified by the regulation, such as the sponsor’s intent to terminate the contract, the effective date of non-renewal, and any other information deemed necessary for CMS to assess the impact on beneficiaries and the Medicare program. The provision is designed to protect the interests of Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage and enabling CMS to take proactive steps, such as facilitating plan transitions or initiating new contract solicitations. While the regulation emphasizes the importance of timely and complete notification, it also reflects a broader policy goal of maintaining stability and accountability in the Medicare Part D program. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by clarifying both the timing (first Monday of June) and the substance (required content and intent to not renew) of the notification process. Overall, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) plays a critical role in the governance of Medicare Part D contracts by ensuring that PDP Sponsors adhere to a standardized, transparent, and timely process when deciding not to renew their agreements with CMS.|1

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

129|INTEGRITY ITEMS|INTEGRITY ITEMS refer to specific instances or findings that involve investigations, legal actions, or arbitration proceedings related to the Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor, its key personnel, subcontractors, or major shareholders. These matters are specifically tied to government payments for healthcare or prescription drug services and may indicate potential misconduct or non-compliance. Such integrity issues are of significant regulatory concern, particularly when they involve allegations of fraud, waste, or abuse in the context of federal healthcare programs. PDP Sponsors are required to disclose any reports or findings related to these integrity matters to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of their compliance obligations. This disclosure requirement ensures transparency and accountability in the administration of government-funded healthcare programs. The scope of INTEGRITY ITEMS encompasses not only direct actions against the PDP Sponsor but also any associated parties—such as subcontractors or major shareholders—whose actions may impact the integrity of the healthcare payment system. The obligation to report these items underscores the importance of maintaining ethical standards and operational compliance within the Medicare Part D program. By requiring timely and accurate disclosure, CMS aims to safeguard public funds, ensure fair distribution of benefits, and uphold the trustworthiness of the healthcare delivery system. Therefore, INTEGRITY ITEMS serve as a critical component of regulatory oversight, enabling CMS to monitor risks, take corrective actions when necessary, and enforce compliance across the PDP ecosystem.|1

2|SOCIAL SECURITY ACT|The Social Security Act is a landmark U.S. federal law enacted in 1935 that established foundational social insurance programs, including Medicare. As a cornerstone of the American social safety net, the Act has been amended over time to expand its scope and address evolving healthcare needs. One of its most significant expansions is the inclusion of Medicare Part D, which provides a voluntary prescription drug benefit to eligible individuals. Sections 1860D-1 through 1860D-43 of the Social Security Act specifically govern the structure, operation, and regulation of the Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit, outlining the framework for private drug plans (PDPs) and their administration. These provisions ensure that PDP Sponsors—private entities contracted to offer Part D plans—operate under strict federal guidelines to maintain quality, accessibility, and cost-effectiveness for beneficiaries.

In addition to its role in establishing and regulating Medicare Part D, the Social Security Act includes critical provisions aimed at safeguarding the integrity of federal healthcare programs. Sections 1128 and 1128A of the Act prohibit individuals and entities who have been excluded from participation in Medicare from being employed or contracted by PDP Sponsors. This exclusionary mechanism is designed to prevent individuals with a history of fraud, abuse, or other misconduct from influencing or participating in the delivery of Medicare benefits, thereby protecting both public funds and patient welfare. These provisions underscore the Act’s broader commitment to accountability and ethical conduct within the healthcare system.

Together, the Social Security Act’s provisions on Part D and program integrity reflect a comprehensive approach to ensuring that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, affordable prescription drug coverage while maintaining the integrity of the federal healthcare system. The Act’s enduring relevance is demonstrated by its continued adaptation to new challenges, including the expansion of drug coverage and the enforcement of compliance standards. As a foundational piece of legislation, the Social Security Act remains central to the functioning of the U.S. healthcare system, particularly in the administration of Medicare and its prescription drug benefit.|7

184|PDP SPONSOR|EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY|PDP SPONSOR and EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY are key entities within the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) framework, governed by federal regulations to ensure program integrity and compliance. PDP Sponsors, which are organizations responsible for administering Medicare Part D plans, are required to maintain strict oversight of their workforce and contracted service providers. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must ensure that no services are provided by individuals or entities that are excluded from participation in Medicare programs, as defined under 42 CFR § 423.752(a)(6). This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors proactively verify the status of all employees and contractors against the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Office of Inspector General (OIG) List of Excluded Individuals/Entities (LEIE). 

An EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY refers to any person or organization that has been barred from participating in federal healthcare programs, including Medicare, due to fraud, abuse, or other misconduct. This exclusion is enforced under the Social Security Act and applies to a wide range of providers, suppliers, and business associates. PDP Sponsors are legally obligated to avoid employing or contracting with any such excluded individuals or entities, as doing so would violate federal law and could result in significant penalties, including fines, exclusion from Medicare participation, and reputational damage.

The responsibility of the PDP Sponsor extends beyond initial hiring or contracting; it includes ongoing monitoring and compliance checks to ensure that excluded individuals or entities are not inadvertently engaged in the delivery of services. This includes conducting regular screenings of all personnel and third-party vendors involved in plan operations, such as pharmacy benefit managers, claims processors, and customer service providers. Failure to comply with these requirements can lead to regulatory action by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), including financial sanctions and mandatory corrective action plans.

In summary, PDP Sponsors must implement robust compliance programs to prevent any association with excluded individuals or entities, ensuring that all services provided under their Medicare Part D plans are delivered by qualified, non-excluded personnel. This obligation is critical to maintaining the integrity of the Medicare program and protecting beneficiaries from potential harm due to unqualified or unethical providers. The relationship between PDP SPONSOR and EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY

3| CFR Part 423 Subpart V.

## D. QUALITY ASSURANCE/UTILIZATION MANAGEMENT

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to operate quality assurance, drug utilization management, drug management, and medication therapy management programs, and to support electronic prescribing, in accordance with Subpart D of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to address and resolve complaints received by CMS against the PDP Sponsor through the CMS complaint tracking system as required in 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(22).
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain a Part D summary plan rating score of at least 3 stars as required by 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(26).
4. PDP Sponsor agrees to pass an essential operations test prior to the start of the benefit year. This provision only applies to new sponsors that have not previously entered into a Part D contract with CMS and neither it, nor another subsidiary of the applicant's parent organization, is offering Part D benefits during the current year. [42 CFR § 423.505(b)(27)]

## E. APPEALS AND GRIEVANCES

PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all requirements in Subpart M of 42 CFR Part 423 governing coverage determinations, grievances and appeals, and formulary exceptions, and the applicable provisions of Subpart U.

## F. PAYMENT TO PDP SPONSOR

1. PDP Sponsor and CMS agree that payment under this contract is governed by the rules in Subpart G of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all applicable Federal laws and regulations, guidance, and authorities pertaining to claims and debt collections. In the event that the Government determines that PDP Sponsor has been overpaid, PDP Sponsor agrees to return those overpaid monies back to the Government.

## G. BID SUBMISSION AND REVIEW

If PDP Sponsor intends to participate in the Part D program for the next program year, PDP Sponsor agrees to submit the next year's bid, including all required information on premiums, benefits, and cost-sharing, by the applicable due date, as provided in Subpart F of 42 CFR Part 423 so that CMS and the PDP Sponsor may conduct negotiations regarding the terms and conditions of the proposed bid and benefit plan renewal.

## H. STATE LAW AND LICENSURE REQUIREMENTS

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with State law to the extent that it is not preempted by Federal law as described in Subpart I of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees that where it is operating in a State using a waiver granted pursuant to 42 CFR § 423.410, such waiver shall be valid for three consecutive program years. PDP Sponsor agrees that expiration of the licensure waiver (and the failure to obtain a license from the relevant State) may be the basis for CMS deleting from PDP Sponsor's service area those PDP

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

7| of the formulary for any purpose related to the administration of the Part D program, including without limitation publicly distributing, displaying, publishing, or reconfiguration of the information in any medium, including www.medicare.gov, and by any electronic, print, or other means of distribution.

## Article IV HIPAA Provisions

- A. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with the confidentiality and enrollee record accuracy requirements specified in 42 CFR § 423.136.
- B. PDP Sponsor agrees to enter into a business associate agreement with the entity with which CMS has contracted to track Medicare beneficiaries' true out-of- pocket costs.

Article V

## Requirements of Other Laws and Regulations

PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with (a) applicable Federal laws and regulations designed to prevent fraud, waste, and abuse, including, but not limited to, applicable provisions of Federal criminal law, the False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. §§ 3729 et seq.), and the anti-kickback provision of § 1128B of the Act; (b) applicable Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) rules at 45 CFR Parts 160, 162, and 164; and (c) all other applicable Federal statutes and regulations.

## Article VI Contract Term and Renewal

## A. TERM OF CONTRACT

This contract is effective from the date of CMS's authorized representative's signature through December 31, 2025. This contract shall be renewable for successive one-year periods thereafter according to 42 CFR § 423.506.

## B. QUALIFICATION TO RENEW A CONTRACT

1. In accordance with 42 CFR § 423.507, PDP Sponsor will be determined qualified to renew its contract annually only if PDP Sponsor has not provided CMS with a notice of intention not to renew in accordance with Article VII of this contract.
2. Although PDP Sponsor may be determined qualified to renew its contract under this Article, if PDP Sponsor and CMS cannot reach agreement on the bid under Subpart F of 42 CFR Part 423 and CMS declines to accept the bid pursuant to 42 CFR § 423.265(b)(4), no renewal takes place, and, in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.502(d)(2), the failure to reach agreement is not subject to the appeals provisions in Subpart N of 42 CFR Part 423.

## Article VII Nonrenewal of Contract by PDP Sponsor

- A. PDP Sponsor may elect not to renew its contract with CMS, effective at the end of the term of the contract for any reason as long as PDP Sponsor provides proper notice of the decision according to the required timeframes. [42 CFR § 423.507(a)(1)]
- B. If PDP Sponsor does not intend to renew its contract, in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.507(a)(2), it must notify:
3. (a
3| CFR Part 423 Subpart V.

## D. QUALITY ASSURANCE/UTILIZATION MANAGEMENT

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to operate quality assurance, drug utilization management, drug management, and medication therapy management programs, and to support electronic prescribing, in accordance with Subpart D of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to address and resolve complaints received by CMS against the PDP Sponsor through the CMS complaint tracking system as required in 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(22).
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain a Part D summary plan rating score of at least 3 stars as required by 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(26).
4. PDP Sponsor agrees to pass an essential operations test prior to the start of the benefit year. This provision only applies to new sponsors that have not previously entered into a Part D contract with CMS and neither it, nor another subsidiary of the applicant's parent organization, is offering Part D benefits during the current year. [42 CFR § 423.505(b)(27)]

24|CMS|The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is the federal agency responsible for administering and overseeing the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Benefit program. CMS oversees the contracting process with PDP Sponsors, ensuring that plans meet federal requirements before entering or renewing contracts. CMS plays a critical role in ensuring compliance with federal regulations under 42 CFR Part 423, enforcing contractual obligations, and monitoring plan performance through complaint tracking and regulatory actions. The agency conducts regular audits and reviews to ensure that PDPs adhere to quality, financial, and operational standards. Additionally, CMS is responsible for the attestation of benefit plans and the renewal of contracts, ensuring that plans continue to meet federal benchmarks and serve beneficiaries effectively.

13|RATE ANNOUNCEMENT|The RATE ANNOUNCEMENT is an official publication issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that provides comprehensive information regarding the approved rates and premium details for Medicare Part D plans for a specific contract year. It includes the annual rate updates and payment adjustments necessary for the upcoming contract year, ensuring transparency and consistency in the financial framework governing prescription drug coverage under Medicare.

The RATE ANNOUNCEMENT outlines key financial components such as the standard monthly premium, the benchmark bid rate, and the reimbursement rates that CMS will use to calculate payments to Part D plans. These rates are determined based on a combination of actuarial analysis, historical claims data, and market trends, and they reflect the anticipated costs of providing prescription drug benefits. Additionally, the announcement may include adjustments related to risk adjustment, low-income cost-sharing (LIS) subsidies, and other program-specific factors that influence plan payments and beneficiary costs.

By issuing the RATE ANNOUNCEMENT annually, CMS ensures that all Part D plan sponsors have timely access to the official rate data required for plan design, enrollment, and financial planning. The publication also supports regulatory compliance and helps maintain the integrity of the Medicare Part D program by standardizing rate calculations across all participating plans. The information contained in the RATE ANNOUNCEMENT is essential for determining beneficiary premiums, plan sponsor payments, and overall program sustainability.

Overall, the RATE ANNOUNCEMENT functions as a foundational document in the Medicare Part D framework, delivering authoritative, up-to-date rate information that enables accurate budgeting, enrollment management, and policy implementation. 154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors maintain strict compliance with data certification, recordkeeping, access protocols, and reporting obligations to ensure transparency, accountability, and the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must certify the accuracy and completeness of data submitted to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), maintain detailed and organized records for specified retention periods, and ensure timely and secure access to these records for regulatory review and audit purposes. Additionally, the regulation emphasizes the importance of fiscal soundness, requiring PDP Sponsors to demonstrate financial stability and the ability to meet their contractual obligations to beneficiaries over the long term. This includes maintaining sufficient reserves and implementing robust financial management practices. Furthermore, 42 CFR § 423.505 requires PDP Sponsors to develop and maintain business continuity plans to ensure uninterrupted service delivery during emergencies or disruptions. These plans must address potential risks such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, or operational failures, and must include strategies for maintaining critical functions, data integrity, and beneficiary access to prescription drugs. The regulation serves as a foundational framework to uphold the reliability, security, and sustainability of Medicare Part D plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive consistent, high-quality pharmaceutical coverage. By integrating data governance, financial responsibility, and operational resilience, 42 CFR § 423.505 plays a critical role in safeguarding the Medicare program’s integrity and protecting the interests of both beneficiaries and the federal government.|1

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. 3| CFR Part 423 Subpart V.

## D. QUALITY ASSURANCE/UTILIZATION MANAGEMENT

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to operate quality assurance, drug utilization management, drug management, and medication therapy management programs, and to support electronic prescribing, in accordance with Subpart D of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to address and resolve complaints received by CMS against the PDP Sponsor through the CMS complaint tracking system as required in 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(22).
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain a Part D summary plan rating score of at least 3 stars as required by 42 CFR § 423.505(b)(26).
4. PDP Sponsor agrees to pass an essential operations test prior to the start of the benefit year. This provision only applies to new sponsors that have not previously entered into a Part D contract with CMS and neither it, nor another subsidiary of the applicant's parent organization, is offering Part D benefits during the current year. [42 CFR § 423.505(b)(27)]

## E. APPEALS AND GRIEVANCES

PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all requirements in Subpart M of 42 CFR Part 423 governing coverage determinations, grievances and appeals, and formulary exceptions, and the applicable provisions of Subpart U.

## F. PAYMENT TO PDP SPONSOR

1. PDP Sponsor and CMS agree that payment under this contract is governed by the rules in Subpart G of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with all applicable Federal laws and regulations, guidance, and authorities pertaining to claims and debt collections. In the event that the Government determines that PDP Sponsor has been overpaid, PDP Sponsor agrees to return those overpaid monies back to the Government.

## G. BID SUBMISSION AND REVIEW

If PDP Sponsor intends to participate in the Part D program for the next program year, PDP Sponsor agrees to submit the next year's bid, including all required information on premiums, benefits, and cost-sharing, by the applicable due date, as provided in Subpart F of 42 CFR Part 423 so that CMS and the PDP Sponsor may conduct negotiations regarding the terms and conditions of the proposed bid and benefit plan renewal.

## H. STATE LAW AND LICENSURE REQUIREMENTS

1. PDP Sponsor agrees to comply with State law to the extent that it is not preempted by Federal law as described in Subpart I of 42 CFR Part 423.
2. PDP Sponsor agrees that where it is operating in a State using a waiver granted pursuant to 42 CFR § 423.410, such waiver shall be valid for three consecutive program years. 25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. The agency also manages contract terminations and approvals for notices, ensuring that changes to plan offerings or sponsorships are properly vetted and compliant with federal law. Through its comprehensive regulatory framework, CMS ensures that the Medicare Part D program remains accessible, affordable, and aligned with federal policy objectives. Overall, CMS serves as the central authority for the administration, regulation, and continuous improvement of the Medicare Part D program, safeguarding the interests of beneficiaries and maintaining the integrity of the prescription drug benefit system.|46
153|MEDICARE PART D PROGRAM|The Medicare Part D Program is a federal initiative designed to provide prescription drug coverage to Medicare beneficiaries. Administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), the program operates under the regulatory framework established in 42 CFR § 423. It is delivered through a network of private plan sponsors known as Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors, who contract with CMS to offer drug coverage options to eligible individuals. These PDP Sponsors are responsible for designing, managing, and offering Medicare Part D plans, which include formularies, cost-sharing structures, and pharmacy networks. The program aims to help beneficiaries manage the cost of prescription medications by offering standardized coverage with varying levels of benefits, premiums, and deductibles. Beneficiaries may enroll in a standalone Part D plan or receive coverage through a Medicare Advantage plan that includes drug benefits. The Medicare Part D Program is a key component of the broader Medicare system, ensuring access to essential medications for seniors and individuals with qualifying disabilities. Through its structured oversight by CMS and reliance on private sector participation via PDP Sponsors, the program balances federal regulation with plan flexibility, enabling beneficiaries to choose plans that best meet their healthcare needs and financial circumstances.|4
17|ADMINISTRATOR OF CMS|The official within the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services responsible for making determinations regarding the eligibility of entities to operate Medicare Prescription Drug Plans. 10|MEDICARE PRESCRIPTION PAYMENT PLAN FINAL GUIDANCE|The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan Final Guidance is a comprehensive regulatory document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that establishes the framework for the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (MPPP). This program enables Medicare beneficiaries to pay for their prescription drugs in manageable monthly installments instead of a single lump-sum payment, thereby improving access to essential medications and reducing financial strain. The final guidance provides detailed rules and operational requirements for both beneficiaries and participating pharmacies, ensuring consistency and clarity in the implementation of the plan. It outlines eligibility criteria, enrollment procedures, payment structures, and the responsibilities of pharmacies in administering the installment payments. 10|MEDICARE PRESCRIPTION PAYMENT PLAN FINAL GUIDANCE|The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan Final Guidance is a comprehensive regulatory document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that establishes the framework for the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (MPPP). This program enables Medicare beneficiaries to pay for their prescription drugs in manageable monthly installments instead of a single lump-sum payment, thereby improving access to essential medications and reducing financial strain. The final guidance provides detailed rules and operational requirements for both beneficiaries and participating pharmacies, ensuring consistency and clarity in the implementation of the plan. It outlines eligibility criteria, enrollment procedures, payment structures, and the responsibilities of pharmacies in administering the installment payments. The guidance also addresses how the plan integrates with existing Medicare Part D coverage, clarifying that the MPPP is an optional benefit that does not alter the standard formulary or coverage rules of Part D. By allowing beneficiaries to spread out their drug costs over time, the program aims to enhance medication adherence and overall health outcomes, particularly for individuals with high out-of-pocket expenses. CMS emphasizes that the final guidance is designed to support a seamless and transparent experience for beneficiaries, with clear communication from pharmacies about payment terms and any potential interest or fees associated with the installment plan. The guidance reflects CMS’s commitment to improving affordability and accessibility in Medicare prescription drug coverage, aligning with broader efforts to reduce financial barriers to care. 55|MEDICATION THERAPY MANAGEMENT|Medication Therapy Management (MTM) is a comprehensive program mandated for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors under Subpart D of Medicare regulations, designed to optimize medication use and enhance patient health outcomes. This service is integral to the quality assurance framework of PDP plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive coordinated, effective, and safe medication care. MTM is delivered by PDP Sponsors as a proactive service aimed at helping beneficiaries manage their medications more effectively, with a focus on improving medication adherence, reducing the risk of adverse drug interactions, and minimizing medication-related problems. The program includes a structured review of a beneficiary’s entire medication regimen, including prescription drugs, over-the-counter medications, and dietary supplements, to identify potential issues such as duplications, omissions, inappropriate dosages, or drug interactions. Through personalized medication action plans, patient education, and collaboration with healthcare providers, MTM supports beneficiaries in making informed decisions about their treatment. The service is particularly targeted at individuals with multiple chronic conditions who are taking multiple medications, as they are at higher risk for medication-related complications. By promoting safe and effective medication use, MTM contributes to improved health outcomes, reduced hospitalizations, and lower overall healthcare costs. The program is not only a regulatory requirement but also a vital component of patient-centered care within the Medicare Part D framework. 25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs).
