189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

183|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the process by which a Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor must notify the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) of its intent not to renew a Medicare Part D contract. Overall, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) plays a critical role in the governance of Medicare Part D contracts by ensuring that PDP Sponsors adhere to a standardized, transparent, and timely process when deciding not to renew their agreements with CMS.|1

154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors maintain strict compliance with data certification, recordkeeping, access protocols, and reporting obligations to ensure transparency, accountability, and the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must certify the accuracy and completeness of data submitted to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), maintain detailed and organized records for specified retention periods, and ensure timely and secure access to these records for regulatory review and audit purposes. Additionally, the regulation emphasizes the importance of fiscal soundness, requiring PDP Sponsors to demonstrate financial stability and the ability to meet their contractual obligations to beneficiaries over the long term. This includes maintaining sufficient reserves and implementing robust financial management practices. Furthermore, 42 CFR § 423.505 requires PDP Sponsors to develop and maintain business continuity plans to ensure uninterrupted service delivery during emergencies or disruptions. These plans must address potential risks such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, or operational failures, and must include strategies for maintaining critical functions, data integrity, and beneficiary access to prescription drugs. The regulation serves as a foundational framework to uphold the reliability, security, and sustainability of Medicare Part D plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive consistent, high-quality pharmaceutical coverage. By integrating data governance, financial responsibility, and operational resilience, 42 CFR § 423.505 plays a critical role in safeguarding the Medicare program’s integrity and protecting the interests of both beneficiaries and the federal government.|1
101|42 CFR § 423.505(B)(18)|A federal regulation requiring PDP Sponsors to contract with any pharmacy meeting reasonable and relevant terms and conditions, including making standard contracts available upon request.|1
172|42 CFR § 423.507|A regulation detailing the conditions under which a PDP Sponsor may or may not renew its contract with CMS, including notice requirements|1
42|42 CFR § 423.505(B)(25)|A regulation mandating that PDP Sponsors maintain sufficient administrative and management capabilities to organize, implement, and control financial, communication, benefit administration, and quality assurance activities related to Part D services.|1
205|42 CFR § 423.505(N)|A regulatory provision that authorizes CMS to take compliance actions or issue intermediate sanctions when a PDP Sponsor is found out of compliance with Part D requirements|1
25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157
47|42 CFR § 423.48|A regulation requiring PDP Sponsors to provide specific information to beneficiaries and CMS, ensuring transparency and compliance with Part D reporting requirements.|1

73|42 CFR § 423.505(B)(22)|A specific regulation within 42 CFR Part 423 that requires PDP Sponsors to address and resolve complaints received by CMS through the CMS complaint tracking system|1

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

By replacing traditional paper-based prescriptions, Electronic Prescribing enhances operational efficiency, reduces administrative burdens, and minimizes the risk of errors associated with handwritten or misinterpreted prescriptions. ## P. CERTIFICATION OF DATA THAT DETERMINE PAYMENT

PDP Sponsor must provide certifications in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(k).

## Q. ENROLLMENT RELATED COSTS

PDP Sponsor agrees to payment of fees established by CMS for cost sharing of enrollment related costs in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.6.

## R. PDP SPONSOR REIMBURSEMENT TO PHARMACIES [42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(21) and 423.520]

1. If a PDP Sponsor uses a standard for reimbursement of pharmacies based on the cost of a drug, PDP Sponsor will update such standard not less frequently than once every 7 days, beginning with an initial update on January 1 of each year, to accurately reflect the market price of the drug.
2. If the source for any prescription drug pricing standard is not publicly available, PDP

Sponsor will disclose all individual drug prices to be updated to the applicable pharmacies in advance for their use for the reimbursement of claims.

3. PDP Sponsor will issue, mail, or otherwise transmit payment with respect to all claims submitted by pharmacies (other than pharmacies that dispense drugs by mail order only, or are located in, or contract with, a long-term care facility) within 14 days of receipt of an electronically submitted claim or within 30 days of receipt of a claim submitted otherwise.
4. PDP Sponsor must ensure that a pharmacy located in, or having a contract with, a longterm care facility will have not less than 30 days (but not more than 90 days) to submit claims to PDP Sponsor for reimbursement.

## Article III Record Retention and Reporting Requirements

## A. RECORD MAINTENANCE AND ACCESS

PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain records and provide access in accordance with 42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(10) and 423.505(i)(2).

## B. GENERAL REPORTING REQUIREMENTS

PDP Sponsor agrees to submit information to CMS according to 42 CFR §§ 423.505(f) and 423.514, and applicable Final Medicare Part D Reporting Guidance.

129|INTEGRITY ITEMS|INTEGRITY ITEMS refer to specific instances or findings that involve investigations, legal actions, or arbitration proceedings related to the Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor, its key personnel, subcontractors, or major shareholders. These matters are specifically tied to government payments for healthcare or prescription drug services and may indicate potential misconduct or non-compliance. Such integrity issues are of significant regulatory concern, particularly when they involve allegations of fraud, waste, or abuse in the context of federal healthcare programs. PDP Sponsors are required to disclose any reports or findings related to these integrity matters to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of their compliance obligations. This disclosure requirement ensures transparency and accountability in the administration of government-funded healthcare programs. The scope of INTEGRITY ITEMS encompasses not only direct actions against the PDP Sponsor but also any associated parties—such as subcontractors or major shareholders—whose actions may impact the integrity of the healthcare payment system. The obligation to report these items underscores the importance of maintaining ethical standards and operational compliance within the Medicare Part D program. By requiring timely and accurate disclosure, CMS aims to safeguard public funds, ensure fair distribution of benefits, and uphold the trustworthiness of the healthcare delivery system. Therefore, INTEGRITY ITEMS serve as a critical component of regulatory oversight, enabling CMS to monitor risks, take corrective actions when necessary, and enforce compliance across the PDP ecosystem.|1

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

139|42 CFR § 423.752(A)(6)|"42 CFR § 423.752(A)(6)" is a provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that outlines specific services and activities prohibited for entities that employ or contract with individuals or organizations excluded from participation in federal healthcare programs. This regulation is particularly relevant to compliance within the Medicare Part D program, which provides prescription drug coverage to beneficiaries. The rule is designed to safeguard the integrity of the program by preventing entities that are associated with excluded individuals or entities from engaging in certain activities that could compromise program oversight, financial accountability, or patient safety.

Under this regulation, entities that contract with Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors are explicitly barred from performing services or engaging in activities involving excluded individuals or entities. Prohibited services may include, but are not limited to, activities related to plan administration, pharmacy network management, claims processing, drug formulary development, and any other function that could influence the delivery or oversight of Medicare Part D benefits. The intent is to ensure that excluded individuals—those who have been barred from participating in federal healthcare programs due to fraud, abuse, or other misconduct—cannot indirectly influence or benefit from the operations of PDP Sponsors through contracted entities.

The regulation reinforces the broader compliance framework of the Medicare Part D program by requiring PDP Sponsors and their contractors to conduct thorough due diligence in verifying the status of individuals and organizations they engage. This includes implementing robust screening processes and monitoring mechanisms to detect and prevent any unauthorized involvement of excluded parties. Violations of "42 CFR § 423.752(A)(6)" can result in significant penalties, including financial sanctions, exclusion from the program, and reputational damage.

In summary, "42 CFR § 423.752(A)(6)" serves as a critical compliance safeguard within the Medicare Part D program, prohibiting entities that contract with PDP Sponsors from engaging in any services or activities involving excluded individuals or entities. By enforcing this restriction, the regulation helps maintain the integrity, accountability, and trustworthiness of the prescription drug benefit system, ensuring that only qualified and compliant parties participate in its administration.|1

There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by detailing both the right to non-renewal and the mandatory procedural safeguards that must be followed. 189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. 129|INTEGRITY ITEMS|INTEGRITY ITEMS refer to specific instances or findings that involve investigations, legal actions, or arbitration proceedings related to the Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor, its key personnel, subcontractors, or major shareholders. These matters are specifically tied to government payments for healthcare or prescription drug services and may indicate potential misconduct or non-compliance. Such integrity issues are of significant regulatory concern, particularly when they involve allegations of fraud, waste, or abuse in the context of federal healthcare programs. PDP Sponsors are required to disclose any reports or findings related to these integrity matters to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of their compliance obligations. This disclosure requirement ensures transparency and accountability in the administration of government-funded healthcare programs. The scope of INTEGRITY ITEMS encompasses not only direct actions against the PDP Sponsor but also any associated parties—such as subcontractors or major shareholders—whose actions may impact the integrity of the healthcare payment system. The obligation to report these items underscores the importance of maintaining ethical standards and operational compliance within the Medicare Part D program. By requiring timely and accurate disclosure, CMS aims to safeguard public funds, ensure fair distribution of benefits, and uphold the trustworthiness of the healthcare delivery system. Therefore, INTEGRITY ITEMS serve as a critical component of regulatory oversight, enabling CMS to monitor risks, take corrective actions when necessary, and enforce compliance across the PDP ecosystem.|1

2. PDP Sponsor agrees to provide notice based on best knowledge, information, and belief to CMS of any integrity items related to payments from governmental entities, both Federal and State, for healthcare or prescription drug services that would have been reported as part of Table A. of the Business Integrity section of the PDP application. These items include any investigations, legal actions or matters subject to arbitration brought involving the PDP Sponsor (or the PDP Sponsor's firm if applicable) and its subcontractors (excluding contracted network providers), including any key management or executive staff, or any major shareholders (5% or more), by a government agency (State or Federal) on matters relating to payments from governmental entities, both Federal and State, for healthcare and/or prescription drug services. In providing the notice, the PDP Sponsor shall keep the Government informed of when the integrity item is initiated and when it is closed. Notice should be provided of the details concerning any resolution and monetary payments as well as any settlement agreements or corporate integrity agreements.
3. PDP Sponsor agrees to provide notice based on best knowledge, information, and belief to CMS in the event PDP Sponsor or any of its subcontractors is criminally convicted or has a civil judgment entered against it for fraudulent activities or is sanctioned under any Federal program involving the provision of health care or prescription drug services.

## L. LOW-INCOME SUBSIDY

PDP Sponsor agrees that it will participate in the administration of subsidies for low-income subsidy eligible individuals according to Subpart P of 42 CFR Part 423.

## M. COMMUNICATION WITH CMS

PDP Sponsor agrees that it shall maintain the capacity to communicate with CMS electronically in accordance with CMS requirements.

## N. BENEFICIARY FINANCIAL PROTECTIONS

PDP Sponsor agrees to afford its enrollees protection from liability for payment of fees that are the obligation of PDP Sponsor in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(g).

## O. RELATIONSHIP WITH FIRST TIER, DOWNSTREAM, AND RELATED ENTITIES

1. PDP Sponsor agrees that it maintains ultimate responsibility for adhering to and otherwise fully complying with all terms and conditions of this contract with CMS. [42 CFR § 423.505(i)]
2. PDP Sponsor shall ensure that any contracts or agreements with first tier, downstream, and related entities performing functions on PDP Sponsor's behalf related to the operation of the Part D benefit are in compliance with 42 CFR § 423.505(i).
3. PDP Sponsor agrees not to employ or contract with an individual or entity who is excluded from participation in Medicare under Section 1128 or 1128A of the Social Security Act (or with an entity that employs or contracts with an excluded individual or entity) for provision of any of the services listed in 42 CFR § 423.752(a)(6).

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

## P. CERTIFICATION OF DATA THAT DETERMINE PAYMENT

PDP Sponsor must provide certifications in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(k).

## Q. ENROLLMENT RELATED COSTS

PDP Sponsor agrees to payment of fees established by CMS for cost sharing of enrollment related costs in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.6.

## R. PDP SPONSOR REIMBURSEMENT TO PHARMACIES [42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(21) and 423.520]

1. If a PDP Sponsor uses a standard for reimbursement of pharmacies based on the cost of a drug, PDP Sponsor will update such standard not less frequently than once every 7 days, beginning with an initial update on January 1 of each year, to accurately reflect the market price of the drug.
2. If the source for any prescription drug pricing standard is not publicly available, PDP

Sponsor will disclose all individual drug prices to be updated to the applicable pharmacies in advance for their use for the reimbursement of claims.

154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors maintain strict compliance with data certification, recordkeeping, access protocols, and reporting obligations to ensure transparency, accountability, and the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must certify the accuracy and completeness of data submitted to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), maintain detailed and organized records for specified retention periods, and ensure timely and secure access to these records for regulatory review and audit purposes. Additionally, the regulation emphasizes the importance of fiscal soundness, requiring PDP Sponsors to demonstrate financial stability and the ability to meet their contractual obligations to beneficiaries over the long term. This includes maintaining sufficient reserves and implementing robust financial management practices. Furthermore, 42 CFR § 423.505 requires PDP Sponsors to develop and maintain business continuity plans to ensure uninterrupted service delivery during emergencies or disruptions. These plans must address potential risks such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, or operational failures, and must include strategies for maintaining critical functions, data integrity, and beneficiary access to prescription drugs. The regulation serves as a foundational framework to uphold the reliability, security, and sustainability of Medicare Part D plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive consistent, high-quality pharmaceutical coverage. By integrating data governance, financial responsibility, and operational resilience, 42 CFR § 423.505 plays a critical role in safeguarding the Medicare program’s integrity and protecting the interests of both beneficiaries and the federal government.|1

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

7|MEDICARE COMMUNICATIONS AND MARKETING GUIDELINES|The Medicare Communications and Marketing Guidelines are federal regulations issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that govern how Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors may communicate with and market their plans to beneficiaries. These guidelines are designed to ensure transparency, accuracy, and compliance with established consumer protection standards. By setting clear parameters for marketing activities, the guidelines aim to protect beneficiaries from misleading or deceptive information, enabling them to make informed decisions about their prescription drug coverage. The rules apply specifically to PDP Sponsors, who are responsible for adhering to these standards when developing and disseminating marketing materials, conducting outreach, and engaging with Medicare beneficiaries through various channels, including print, digital, and in-person communications. The guidelines emphasize truthful, balanced, and easily understandable information, requiring that all plan benefits, costs, formulary details, and coverage limitations be presented clearly and without exaggeration. Additionally, the regulations prohibit the use of tactics that could mislead or manipulate beneficiaries, such as omitting key information, using confusing terminology, or making unsubstantiated claims. CMS enforces these guidelines to maintain the integrity of the Medicare Part D program and to promote equitable access to accurate health information. Compliance with the Medicare Communications and Marketing Guidelines is mandatory for all PDP Sponsors, and failure to adhere may result in penalties or corrective actions. Overall, the guidelines serve as a critical framework for ensuring that marketing efforts are ethical, compliant, and focused on the best interests of Medicare beneficiaries.|2
64|BENEFICIARY PREMIUMS|Monthly payments made by Medicare Part D enrollees, which PDP Sponsors are responsible for calculating and collecting in accordance with federal regulations.|1
9|MEDICARE PART D MANUFACTURER DISCOUNT PROGRAM FINAL GUIDANCE|The Medicare Part D Manufacturer Discount Program Final Guidance is a comprehensive federal regulatory document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that establishes the rules and requirements for the Medicare Part D Manufacturer Discount Program. This program mandates that pharmaceutical manufacturers provide discounts on certain prescription drugs to Medicare beneficiaries enrolled in Part D prescription drug plans. The final guidance outlines the specific criteria, calculation methodologies, and compliance standards that manufacturers must follow to ensure eligible beneficiaries receive the required discounts. It plays a critical role in shaping drug pricing and rebate structures within the Medicare Part D framework, aiming to reduce out-of-pocket costs for beneficiaries and improve access to essential medications. The guidance clarifies how manufacturers should report and apply discounts, ensuring transparency and consistency across the program. By setting clear implementation protocols, the final guidance supports CMS’s broader mission to enhance affordability and efficiency in Medicare drug coverage. The program is designed to align with the intent of the Affordable Care Act and subsequent legislative efforts to control prescription drug costs. Overall, the Medicare Part D Manufacturer Discount Program Final Guidance serves as a foundational regulatory tool that governs manufacturer responsibilities, influences drug pricing dynamics, and strengthens the financial protection of Medicare beneficiaries.|3
11|CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES|The CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES are official documents issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to assist plan sponsors, beneficiaries, and other stakeholders in understanding and complying with the requirements of the Medicare Part D program. These guides serve as essential resources for PDP (Prescription Drug Plan) Sponsors and other participants, offering detailed explanations of operational procedures, policy interpretations, and regulatory expectations. Designed to promote consistency and clarity, the CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES provide authoritative guidance on program administration, including enrollment processes, formulary management, drug pricing, coverage determinations, and appeals procedures. They also address key compliance issues, helping sponsors navigate complex regulatory frameworks and ensure adherence to federal standards. By clarifying CMS policies and procedures, these documents support accurate implementation of Medicare Part D benefits, reduce administrative errors, and enhance the overall effectiveness of the prescription drug program. The guides are regularly updated to reflect changes in legislation, regulations, and program operations, ensuring that stakeholders have access to current and reliable information. As official CMS publications, the CMS PARTICIPANT GUIDES are recognized as authoritative sources for interpreting Medicare Part D rules and are widely used by plan sponsors, pharmacy networks, healthcare providers, and beneficiaries alike. Their comprehensive nature makes them indispensable tools for maintaining program integrity, improving beneficiary access to medications, and supporting the smooth functioning of the Medicare Part D framework.|2

154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors maintain strict compliance with data certification, recordkeeping, access protocols, and reporting obligations to ensure transparency, accountability, and the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must certify the accuracy and completeness of data submitted to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), maintain detailed and organized records for specified retention periods, and ensure timely and secure access to these records for regulatory review and audit purposes. Additionally, the regulation emphasizes the importance of fiscal soundness, requiring PDP Sponsors to demonstrate financial stability and the ability to meet their contractual obligations to beneficiaries over the long term. This includes maintaining sufficient reserves and implementing robust financial management practices. Furthermore, 42 CFR § 423.505 requires PDP Sponsors to develop and maintain business continuity plans to ensure uninterrupted service delivery during emergencies or disruptions. These plans must address potential risks such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, or operational failures, and must include strategies for maintaining critical functions, data integrity, and beneficiary access to prescription drugs. The regulation serves as a foundational framework to uphold the reliability, security, and sustainability of Medicare Part D plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive consistent, high-quality pharmaceutical coverage. By integrating data governance, financial responsibility, and operational resilience, 42 CFR § 423.505 plays a critical role in safeguarding the Medicare program’s integrity and protecting the interests of both beneficiaries and the federal government.|1

This individual holds authority under 42 CFR § 423.503 to approve or deny PDP Sponsor status.|1
189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1
211|42 CFR § 423.508(F)|A regulatory provision defining "covered persons" for PDP Sponsors, which determines eligibility for contract continuation and transfer of services in the event of termination|2
183|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the process by which a Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor must notify the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) of its intent not to renew a Medicare Part D contract. This regulation establishes a mandatory notification requirement, ensuring transparency and timely communication between PDP Sponsors and CMS regarding contract non-renewal. The rule specifies that a PDP Sponsor must submit formal notice to CMS by the first Monday of June each year, which serves as the deadline for communicating its decision not to renew the contract for the upcoming plan year. This timing allows CMS sufficient lead time to manage the transition process, ensure continuity of coverage for beneficiaries, and facilitate the necessary administrative and operational adjustments. The notice must include all required content as specified by the regulation, such as the sponsor’s intent to terminate the contract, the effective date of non-renewal, and any other information deemed necessary for CMS to assess the impact on beneficiaries and the Medicare program. The provision is designed to protect the interests of Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage and enabling CMS to take proactive steps, such as facilitating plan transitions or initiating new contract solicitations. While the regulation emphasizes the importance of timely and complete notification, it also reflects a broader policy goal of maintaining stability and accountability in the Medicare Part D program. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by clarifying both the timing (first Monday of June) and the substance (required content and intent to not renew) of the notification process. Overall, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) plays a critical role in the governance of Medicare Part D contracts by ensuring that PDP Sponsors adhere to a standardized, transparent, and timely process when deciding not to renew their agreements with CMS.|1

10|MEDICARE PRESCRIPTION PAYMENT PLAN FINAL GUIDANCE|The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan Final Guidance is a comprehensive regulatory document issued by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) that establishes the framework for the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (MPPP). This program enables Medicare beneficiaries to pay for their prescription drugs in manageable monthly installments instead of a single lump-sum payment, thereby improving access to essential medications and reducing financial strain. The final guidance provides detailed rules and operational requirements for both beneficiaries and participating pharmacies, ensuring consistency and clarity in the implementation of the plan. It outlines eligibility criteria, enrollment procedures, payment structures, and the responsibilities of pharmacies in administering the installment payments. The guidance also addresses how the plan integrates with existing Medicare Part D coverage, clarifying that the MPPP is an optional benefit that does not alter the standard formulary or coverage rules of Part D. By allowing beneficiaries to spread out their drug costs over time, the program aims to enhance medication adherence and overall health outcomes, particularly for individuals with high out-of-pocket expenses. CMS emphasizes that the final guidance is designed to support a seamless and transparent experience for beneficiaries, with clear communication from pharmacies about payment terms and any potential interest or fees associated with the installment plan. The guidance reflects CMS’s commitment to improving affordability and accessibility in Medicare prescription drug coverage, aligning with broader efforts to reduce financial barriers to care. Overall, the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan Final Guidance serves as a critical resource for stakeholders, ensuring that the program operates efficiently, equitably, and in compliance with federal regulations.|2

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157

## P. CERTIFICATION OF DATA THAT DETERMINE PAYMENT

PDP Sponsor must provide certifications in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(k).

## Q. ENROLLMENT RELATED COSTS

PDP Sponsor agrees to payment of fees established by CMS for cost sharing of enrollment related costs in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.6.

## R. PDP SPONSOR REIMBURSEMENT TO PHARMACIES [42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(21) and 423.520]

1. If a PDP Sponsor uses a standard for reimbursement of pharmacies based on the cost of a drug, PDP Sponsor will update such standard not less frequently than once every 7 days, beginning with an initial update on January 1 of each year, to accurately reflect the market price of the drug.
2. If the source for any prescription drug pricing standard is not publicly available, PDP

Sponsor will disclose all individual drug prices to be updated to the applicable pharmacies in advance for their use for the reimbursement of claims.

3. PDP Sponsor will issue, mail, or otherwise transmit payment with respect to all claims submitted by pharmacies (other than pharmacies that dispense drugs by mail order only, or are located in, or contract with, a long-term care facility) within 14 days of receipt of an electronically submitted claim or within 30 days of receipt of a claim submitted otherwise.
4. PDP Sponsor must ensure that a pharmacy located in, or having a contract with, a longterm care facility will have not less than 30 days (but not more than 90 days) to submit claims to PDP Sponsor for reimbursement.

## Article III Record Retention and Reporting Requirements

## A. RECORD MAINTENANCE AND ACCESS

PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain records and provide access in accordance with 42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(10) and 423.505(i)(2).

## B. GENERAL REPORTING REQUIREMENTS

PDP Sponsor agrees to submit information to CMS according to 42 CFR §§ 423.505(f) and 423.514, and applicable Final Medicare Part D Reporting Guidance.

138|42 CFR § 423.505(I)|A regulatory provision mandating that PDP Sponsors maintain ultimate responsibility for compliance with all contract terms and ensure subcontractor compliance|1
154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors maintain strict compliance with data certification, recordkeeping, access protocols, and reporting obligations to ensure transparency, accountability, and the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must certify the accuracy and completeness of data submitted to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), maintain detailed and organized records for specified retention periods, and ensure timely and secure access to these records for regulatory review and audit purposes. Additionally, the regulation emphasizes the importance of fiscal soundness, requiring PDP Sponsors to demonstrate financial stability and the ability to meet their contractual obligations to beneficiaries over the long term. This includes maintaining sufficient reserves and implementing robust financial management practices. Furthermore, 42 CFR § 423.505 requires PDP Sponsors to develop and maintain business continuity plans to ensure uninterrupted service delivery during emergencies or disruptions. These plans must address potential risks such as natural disasters, cyberattacks, or operational failures, and must include strategies for maintaining critical functions, data integrity, and beneficiary access to prescription drugs. The regulation serves as a foundational framework to uphold the reliability, security, and sustainability of Medicare Part D plans, ensuring that beneficiaries receive consistent, high-quality pharmaceutical coverage. 25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157
153|MEDICARE PART D PROGRAM|The Medicare Part D Program is a federal initiative designed to provide prescription drug coverage to Medicare beneficiaries. Administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), the program operates under the regulatory framework established in 42 CFR § 423. It is delivered through a network of private plan sponsors known as Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors, who contract with CMS to offer drug coverage options to eligible individuals. These PDP Sponsors are responsible for designing, managing, and offering Medicare Part D plans, which include formularies, cost-sharing structures, and pharmacy networks. The program aims to help beneficiaries manage the cost of prescription medications by offering standardized coverage with varying levels of benefits, premiums, and deductibles. Beneficiaries may enroll in a standalone Part D plan or receive coverage through a Medicare Advantage plan that includes drug benefits. The Medicare Part D Program is a key component of the broader Medicare system, ensuring access to essential medications for seniors and individuals with qualifying disabilities. Through its structured oversight by CMS and reliance on private sector participation via PDP Sponsors, the program balances federal regulation with plan flexibility, enabling beneficiaries to choose plans that best meet their healthcare needs and financial circumstances.|4

PDP Sponsor must provide certifications in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.505(k).

PDP Sponsor agrees to payment of fees established by CMS for cost sharing of enrollment related costs in accordance with 42 CFR § 423.6.

## R. PDP SPONSOR REIMBURSEMENT TO PHARMACIES [42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(21) and 423.520]

1. If a PDP Sponsor uses a standard for reimbursement of pharmacies based on the cost of a drug, PDP Sponsor will update such standard not less frequently than once every 7 days, beginning with an initial update on January 1 of each year, to accurately reflect the market price of the drug.
2. If the source for any prescription drug pricing standard is not publicly available, PDP

Sponsor will disclose all individual drug prices to be updated to the applicable pharmacies in advance for their use for the reimbursement of claims.

3. PDP Sponsor will issue, mail, or otherwise transmit payment with respect to all claims submitted by pharmacies (other than pharmacies that dispense drugs by mail order only, or are located in, or contract with, a long-term care facility) within 14 days of receipt of an electronically submitted claim or within 30 days of receipt of a claim submitted otherwise.
4. PDP Sponsor must ensure that a pharmacy located in, or having a contract with, a longterm care facility will have not less than 30 days (but not more than 90 days) to submit claims to PDP Sponsor for reimbursement.

PDP Sponsor agrees to maintain records and provide access in accordance with 42 CFR §§ 423.505(b)(10) and 423.505(i)(2).

PDP Sponsor agrees to submit information to CMS according to 42 CFR §§ 423.505(f) and 423.514, and applicable Final Medicare Part D Reporting Guidance.

184|PDP SPONSOR|EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY|PDP SPONSOR and EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY are key entities within the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) framework, governed by federal regulations to ensure program integrity and compliance. PDP Sponsors, which are organizations responsible for administering Medicare Part D plans, are required to maintain strict oversight of their workforce and contracted service providers. Specifically, PDP Sponsors must ensure that no services are provided by individuals or entities that are excluded from participation in Medicare programs, as defined under 42 CFR § 423.752(a)(6). This regulation mandates that PDP Sponsors proactively verify the status of all employees and contractors against the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Office of Inspector General (OIG) List of Excluded Individuals/Entities (LEIE). 

An EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY refers to any person or organization that has been barred from participating in federal healthcare programs, including Medicare, due to fraud, abuse, or other misconduct. This exclusion is enforced under the Social Security Act and applies to a wide range of providers, suppliers, and business associates. PDP Sponsors are legally obligated to avoid employing or contracting with any such excluded individuals or entities, as doing so would violate federal law and could result in significant penalties, including fines, exclusion from Medicare participation, and reputational damage.

The responsibility of the PDP Sponsor extends beyond initial hiring or contracting; it includes ongoing monitoring and compliance checks to ensure that excluded individuals or entities are not inadvertently engaged in the delivery of services. This includes conducting regular screenings of all personnel and third-party vendors involved in plan operations, such as pharmacy benefit managers, claims processors, and customer service providers. Failure to comply with these requirements can lead to regulatory action by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), including financial sanctions and mandatory corrective action plans.

In summary, PDP Sponsors must implement robust compliance programs to prevent any association with excluded individuals or entities, ensuring that all services provided under their Medicare Part D plans are delivered by qualified, non-excluded personnel. This obligation is critical to maintaining the integrity of the Medicare program and protecting beneficiaries from potential harm due to unqualified or unethical providers. The relationship between PDP SPONSOR and EXCLUDED INDIVIDUAL OR ENTITY is defined by strict regulatory boundaries, with the PDP Sponsor bearing the responsibility to enforce these boundaries through proactive screening, monitoring, and adherence to federal exclusion rules.|9.0|13

154|42 CFR § 423.505|42 CFR § 423.505 is a federal regulation under Title 42 of the Code of Federal Regulations that establishes comprehensive requirements for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors operating within the Medicare program. 183|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the process by which a Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor must notify the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) of its intent not to renew a Medicare Part D contract. This regulation establishes a mandatory notification requirement, ensuring transparency and timely communication between PDP Sponsors and CMS regarding contract non-renewal. The rule specifies that a PDP Sponsor must submit formal notice to CMS by the first Monday of June each year, which serves as the deadline for communicating its decision not to renew the contract for the upcoming plan year. This timing allows CMS sufficient lead time to manage the transition process, ensure continuity of coverage for beneficiaries, and facilitate the necessary administrative and operational adjustments. The notice must include all required content as specified by the regulation, such as the sponsor’s intent to terminate the contract, the effective date of non-renewal, and any other information deemed necessary for CMS to assess the impact on beneficiaries and the Medicare program. The provision is designed to protect the interests of Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage and enabling CMS to take proactive steps, such as facilitating plan transitions or initiating new contract solicitations. While the regulation emphasizes the importance of timely and complete notification, it also reflects a broader policy goal of maintaining stability and accountability in the Medicare Part D program. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by clarifying both the timing (first Monday of June) and the substance (required content and intent to not renew) of the notification process. Overall, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) plays a critical role in the governance of Medicare Part D contracts by ensuring that PDP Sponsors adhere to a standardized, transparent, and timely process when deciding not to renew their agreements with CMS.|1
182|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1) is a regulation governing the renewal and termination of contracts for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors under the Medicare program. This provision outlines the conditions under which a PDP Sponsor may elect not to renew its contract at the end of the contract term. Specifically, the regulation permits a PDP Sponsor to decline renewal, provided it complies with strict notification requirements. These requirements mandate that the sponsor must notify both the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) and Medicare enrollees of its intent not to renew the contract. The regulation specifies precise timing for such notifications, ensuring that enrollees are informed well in advance of the contract’s expiration to allow for adequate time to make alternative coverage decisions. Additionally, the content of the notice must meet defined standards, including clear communication of the non-renewal decision, the effective date of termination, and information about available enrollment options during the subsequent Medicare Open Enrollment Period. The purpose of these requirements is to protect the continuity of care for Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage. By enforcing timely and transparent communication, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1) ensures that both CMS and enrollees are adequately informed, enabling a smooth transition for affected individuals. The regulation thus balances the operational flexibility of PDP Sponsors with the critical need to safeguard beneficiary access to essential medications. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by detailing both the right to non-renewal and the mandatory procedural safeguards that must be followed. Together, these elements form a coherent framework that supports regulatory compliance, beneficiary protection, and the stability of the Medicare Part D program.|1
203|42 CFR § 423.509|A regulatory provision that allows CMS to terminate a contract with a PDP Sponsor under specific conditions, as part of compliance and enforcement actions|1
189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

This requirement ensures that enrollees have access to a structured payment option that helps manage the cost of their prescription medications. By replacing traditional paper-based prescriptions, Electronic Prescribing enhances operational efficiency, reduces administrative burdens, and minimizes the risk of errors associated with handwritten or misinterpreted prescriptions. 183|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the process by which a Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsor must notify the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) of its intent not to renew a Medicare Part D contract. This regulation establishes a mandatory notification requirement, ensuring transparency and timely communication between PDP Sponsors and CMS regarding contract non-renewal. The rule specifies that a PDP Sponsor must submit formal notice to CMS by the first Monday of June each year, which serves as the deadline for communicating its decision not to renew the contract for the upcoming plan year. This timing allows CMS sufficient lead time to manage the transition process, ensure continuity of coverage for beneficiaries, and facilitate the necessary administrative and operational adjustments. The notice must include all required content as specified by the regulation, such as the sponsor’s intent to terminate the contract, the effective date of non-renewal, and any other information deemed necessary for CMS to assess the impact on beneficiaries and the Medicare program. The provision is designed to protect the interests of Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage and enabling CMS to take proactive steps, such as facilitating plan transitions or initiating new contract solicitations. While the regulation emphasizes the importance of timely and complete notification, it also reflects a broader policy goal of maintaining stability and accountability in the Medicare Part D program. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by clarifying both the timing (first Monday of June) and the substance (required content and intent to not renew) of the notification process. Overall, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(2) plays a critical role in the governance of Medicare Part D contracts by ensuring that PDP Sponsors adhere to a standardized, transparent, and timely process when deciding not to renew their agreements with CMS.|1
191|FIRST MONDAY OF JUNE|A specific date deadline in the year when the current contract period ends, by which a PDP Sponsor must notify CMS of its intent not to renew the contract|1
182|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1)|42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1) is a regulation governing the renewal and termination of contracts for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors under the Medicare program. This provision outlines the conditions under which a PDP Sponsor may elect not to renew its contract at the end of the contract term. Specifically, the regulation permits a PDP Sponsor to decline renewal, provided it complies with strict notification requirements. These requirements mandate that the sponsor must notify both the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) and Medicare enrollees of its intent not to renew the contract. The regulation specifies precise timing for such notifications, ensuring that enrollees are informed well in advance of the contract’s expiration to allow for adequate time to make alternative coverage decisions. Additionally, the content of the notice must meet defined standards, including clear communication of the non-renewal decision, the effective date of termination, and information about available enrollment options during the subsequent Medicare Open Enrollment Period. The purpose of these requirements is to protect the continuity of care for Medicare beneficiaries by minimizing disruptions in prescription drug coverage. By enforcing timely and transparent communication, 42 CFR § 423.507(A)(1) ensures that both CMS and enrollees are adequately informed, enabling a smooth transition for affected individuals. The regulation thus balances the operational flexibility of PDP Sponsors with the critical need to safeguard beneficiary access to essential medications. There are no contradictions in the provided descriptions; rather, they complement each other by detailing both the right to non-renewal and the mandatory procedural safeguards that must be followed. Together, these elements form a coherent framework that supports regulatory compliance, beneficiary protection, and the stability of the Medicare Part D program.|1

189|42 CFR § 423.508|42 CFR § 423.508 is a regulatory provision within the Code of Federal Regulations that governs the processes and requirements related to contract modifications and terminations for Part D Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) Sponsors participating in the Medicare program. This regulation establishes a formal framework for notifying Medicare enrollees when contract changes are made by mutual agreement between the PDP Sponsor and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The notification process is designed to ensure transparency and informed decision-making by beneficiaries, requiring timely communication about any modifications that may affect plan benefits, premiums, or coverage terms. The regulation emphasizes the importance of clear, accessible, and timely disclosure to protect enrollee interests and maintain trust in the Medicare Part D program.

In addition to governing contract modifications, 42 CFR § 423.508 also outlines the consequences of a PDP Sponsor voluntarily terminating its contract with CMS. If a PDP Sponsor terminates its contract, the regulation imposes a two-year restriction on the sponsor’s ability to reapply to offer prescription drug plans in the same geographic region. This restriction serves as a safeguard to ensure stability and continuity of coverage for Medicare beneficiaries in affected areas. Furthermore, the regulation includes provisions that limit CMS’s ability to enter into new contracts or approve expansions in that region during the same two-year period. This restriction is intended to prevent market disruption and ensure that the transition of plan offerings is managed responsibly, allowing time for alternative sponsors to step in and maintain access to coverage.

Together, these components of 42 CFR § 423.508 reflect a balanced regulatory approach that prioritizes beneficiary protection, administrative accountability, and market stability. By mandating clear notification procedures for contract changes and imposing meaningful consequences for contract terminations, the regulation reinforces the integrity of the Medicare Part D program. It ensures that both PDP Sponsors and CMS act with due consideration for the impact of their decisions on enrollees and regional plan availability. The provision thus plays a critical role in maintaining the reliability and continuity of prescription drug coverage for millions of Medicare beneficiaries across the United States.|1

43|PDP SPONSOR|MEDICARE PART D|PDP Sponsors, operating under the Medicare Part D program, are responsible for delivering the prescription drug benefit to Medicare beneficiaries in accordance with federal guidelines. These sponsors administer all aspects of the Medicare Part D program, including the design and management of prescription drug plans, the determination of benefits, the setting of premiums, and the provision of enrollee services. As key participants in the Medicare Part D framework, PDP Sponsors must comply with the regulatory requirements established by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to ensure plan integrity, beneficiary access, and program accountability. They offer a range of plan options to beneficiaries, enabling individuals to choose coverage that best meets their medication needs and budget. The responsibilities of a PDP Sponsor extend beyond plan design to include claims processing, pharmacy network management, formulary development, and customer support services. By operating within the structured regulatory environment of Medicare Part D, PDP Sponsors play a critical role in ensuring that Medicare beneficiaries have reliable access to affordable prescription medications. Their activities are closely monitored to maintain compliance with federal standards, promote transparency, and support the overall goals of the Medicare Part D program, which include improving medication adherence, reducing out-of-pocket costs, and enhancing the quality of care for seniors and individuals with disabilities.|27.0|17

25|PDP SPONSOR|The PDP Sponsor is a private organization or entity contracted by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to administer Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs). As a key participant in the Medicare program, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for providing prescription drug benefits to Medicare beneficiaries within approved service areas. This includes managing all aspects of the plan’s operation, such as calculating and collecting beneficiary premiums, administering drug benefits, and ensuring access to manufacturer discounts. The PDP Sponsor must comply with a comprehensive set of federal regulations, including those outlined in 42 CFR §§ 423.505, 423.520, and 423.508, as well as requirements under the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) and other applicable laws such as HIPAA and fraud prevention statutes.

The PDP Sponsor is accountable for maintaining accurate records, submitting required reports to CMS, and ensuring ongoing compliance with contract terms, including provisions related to contract renewal, nonrenewal, and data transfer. The organization must also uphold quality assurance standards, manage drug utilization, support electronic prescribing initiatives, and meet performance benchmarks—such as maintaining a minimum 3-star plan rating—to ensure high-quality service delivery. Additionally, the PDP Sponsor is responsible for managing relationships with subcontractors and related entities, ensuring that all partners adhere to federal compliance standards.

The PDP Sponsor plays a central role in the administration of the Medicare Part D program, overseeing reimbursement to pharmacies, monitoring plan performance, and reporting integrity issues to CMS. As a federally regulated entity, the PDP Sponsor must operate transparently and ethically, with a focus on beneficiary access, plan integrity, and regulatory adherence. Through its contractual relationship with CMS, the PDP Sponsor ensures that Medicare beneficiaries receive reliable, compliant, and efficient prescription drug coverage in accordance with federal guidelines and program objectives.|157
